section.title: The Garden
section.sectionRationale: This is the second movement of the toledot of the heavens and the earth, which opens at 2:4. After the wide creation prologue, the author zooms in to ground level, retelling the making of the man and the woman within a garden God plants, and setting up the choice that chapter three will test.
section.thesis: The personal covenant God forms man from dust, breathes life into him, and settles him in a garden-home with work, a loving boundary, and a bride.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: the man formed from the ground; the garden and its rivers; the one command; and the making of a suitable helper.

verses.text: This is the account of the heavens and the earth when they were created, in the day that the {{yhwh|LORD}} God made them.
Now no shrub of the field had yet appeared on the earth, nor had any plant of the field sprouted, for the LORD God had not yet sent rain upon the earth, and there was no man to cultivate the ground.
But springs welled up from the earth and watered the whole surface of the ground.
Then the LORD God formed {{adam|man}} from the dust of the {{adamah|ground}} and breathed the breath of life into his nostrils, and the man became a {{nephesh|living being}}.

media.alt: The garden of Eden with a central tree and four rivers
media.caption: A garden God plants, watered by a river that branches into four.

verses.text: And the LORD God planted a garden in {{eden|Eden}}, in the east, where He placed the man He had formed.

Out of the ground the LORD God gave growth to every tree that is pleasing to the eye and good for food. And in the middle of the garden were the tree of life and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
Now a river flowed out of Eden to water the garden, and from there it branched into four headwaters:
The name of the first river is the Pishon; it winds through the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold.
And the gold of that land is pure, and bdellium and onyx are found there.
The name of the second river is the Gihon; it winds through the whole land of Cush.
The name of the third river is the Tigris; it runs along the east side of Assyria. And the fourth river is the Euphrates.
Then the LORD God took the man and placed him in the Garden of Eden to cultivate and keep it.
And the LORD God commanded him, “You may eat freely from every tree of the garden,
but you must not eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil; for in the day that you eat of it, you will surely die.”

media.caption: Two brought together as one flesh.

verses.text: The LORD God also said, “It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make for him a {{ezer|suitable helper}}.”

And out of the ground the LORD God formed every beast of the field and every bird of the air, and He brought them to the man to see what he would name each one. And whatever the man called each living creature, that was its name.
The man gave names to all the livestock, to the birds of the air, and to every beast of the field. But for Adam no suitable helper was found.
So the LORD God caused the man to fall into a deep sleep, and while he slept, He took one of the man’s ribs and closed up the area with flesh.
And from the rib that the LORD God had taken from the man, He made a woman and brought her to him.
And the man said: “This is now bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh; she shall be called ‘woman,’ for out of man she was taken.”
For this reason a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and they will become one flesh.
And the man and his wife were both naked, and they were not ashamed.

yhwh.gloss: LORD in small capitals stands for the covenant name of God, YHWH. From 2:4 the personal, covenant-keeping God draws near, not only Elohim the mighty Creator of chapter one.

adam.gloss: Man, and also the name Adam. It echoes adamah, ground, so the word itself says that man is taken from the earth he is made to tend.

adamah.gloss: Ground or soil. Man (adam) is formed from the ground (adamah); we are earthy creatures, given dignity only by the breath of God.

nephesh.gloss: A living being, a living soul. The breath of God makes the man a nephesh; life is a gift held from God, not a possession man owns.

eden.gloss: Delight or pleasure. Eden is a garden of God's own planting, the first place where God dwells with man, a pattern of the sanctuary to come.

ezer.gloss: Helper or ally. The same word is used of God Himself as Israel's helper, for example in Psalm 121; it speaks of strength brought alongside, never of inferiority.

basic.context: After the wide view of the seven days, Genesis zooms in. It retells the making of the man and the woman up close, in a garden God plants, so we see how personal the Creator is.
basic.hermeneutics: This is not a second, competing creation story; it is a close-up of day six. Some people read it as a contradiction, but as a zoom-in it fits chapter one. The command about the tree sets up the choice that comes in chapter three.
basic.originalLanguages: God's covenant name, the LORD, appears here. Man (adam) is drawn from the ground (adamah). And the woman is called a suitable helper (ezer), a strong word used even of God helping us, not a word for someone lesser.
basic.typology: The garden where God walks with people points forward to the temple and finally to the new creation. Adam, given work and a bride, points to Jesus, the true Man who wins a bride, the church.
basic.christ: Adam is a pattern of Christ. The deep sleep from which a bride is formed hints at Christ, from whose pierced side the church is born, and marriage itself pictures Christ and the church.
basic.god: God is personal and near: He forms, breathes, plants, commands, and provides. He is generous, you may eat freely; truthful, the warning is real; and kind, it is not good for the man to be alone.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 5 reads marriage as a picture of Christ and the church. Revelation 22 brings back the tree of life and the river. Jesus quotes this chapter on marriage in Matthew 19.
basic.oneStory: God settles His image-bearer in a garden-home with work, a boundary, and a bride. This is the good world that the next chapter will fracture, and that God will labour through the whole Bible to restore.

context.literary: The second panel of the toledot of the heavens and the earth, 2:4 to 4:26. It moves from the ordered cosmos of chapter one to a ground-level scene and, from 2:4, uses the covenant name the LORD God.
context.historical: Written to Israel, whose life centred on a land, a law, and God's presence. Eden reads as the first sanctuary, a pattern their tabernacle and temple would later echo.
context.canonical: The bridge between creation and fall. It establishes the man's calling, the one prohibition, and marriage, each of which chapter three will put to the test.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the Creator as a personal, covenant God who forms man for communion, work, and marriage within the boundary of a loving command.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes God's acts. The one flesh pattern of 2:24 is treated as prescriptive by Jesus and Paul.

debates.question: Is this a second creation account that contradicts chapter one?

views.label: Contradiction
views.case: Some read the different order of events, plants and animals in relation to man, as a rival account from another source.

views.label: Complementary zoom-in
views.case: The chapter is a topical close-up of day six, not a strict sequence, and fits chapter one.

debates.bearing: The complementary reading is the historic Christian view and does not touch the unity or truth of the text.

study.originalLanguages: The load bearing words move together: the covenant name the LORD draws near; the adam and adamah wordplay roots the man in the very earth he is to tend; nephesh marks his life as God's own breath; and ezer names the woman as a strong helper, the word Scripture uses of God Himself.

typology.fulfillment: The garden where God dwells with man prefigures the tabernacle, the temple, and the new creation, Revelation 21 to 22.

typology.type: The deep sleep and the bride from his side
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadows Christ, from whose pierced side the church, His bride, is brought forth.
Barred after the fall, it is restored in the New Jerusalem, Revelation 22:2.

study.christ: Paul calls Adam a type of the One to come, Romans 5:14, and names Christ the last Adam who becomes a life-giving spirit, 1 Corinthians 15:45. The one flesh union of 2:24 is, Paul says, a profound mystery about Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31 to 32. Jesus grounds marriage in this text, Matthew 19:4 to 6.
study.god: The LORD God is intimate and hands-on: forming from dust, breathing life, planting a garden, giving good work, setting a loving limit, and attending to what is not yet good. He is generous, truthful, and personal.

crossReferences.note: The one flesh of marriage is a picture of Christ and the church.
Adam is a type of the One to come.
The river and the tree of life return in the new creation.

study.oneStory: God plants a garden-sanctuary and settles His image-bearer there with work, a word to obey, and a bride. It is the height of the good creation, the very good that chapter three will lose, and what the whole Bible then strains to see restored in Christ and the new Eden.

sermon.title: The First Wedding Day (Genesis 2:18 to 25)
sermon.why: Keller shows how the first marriage in Genesis 2 opens the whole storyline of the Bible, which ends with the wedding of the Lamb, and draws out marriage as a picture of the gospel. A clear, orthodox treatment of this passage.

quiz.q: Why does Genesis 2 tell the making of man again after chapter 1?

options.text: It is a close-up of day six, zooming in on the man, the garden, and the woman.
options.note: Chapter 2 is a topical zoom on day six, not a rival account.

options.text: To add details like the garden and the naming of the animals.
options.note: True detail, but it misses that the chapter is a zoom-in on day six.

options.text: Because it is a separate, later creation by a different author.
options.note: This overreads source theories against the unity of the text.

options.text: Because chapter 1 was a mistake being corrected.
options.note: The chapters complement; they do not correct one another.

quiz.q: What does the word for suitable helper, ezer, tell us about the woman?

options.text: She is a strong helper brought alongside; the same word is used of God helping His people.
options.note: Ezer implies strength and alliance, used even of God, never inferiority.

options.text: She is a companion so that the man is not alone.
options.note: True, but it misses the strength the word carries.

options.text: She is an assistant of lesser standing.
options.note: The word does not imply lesser worth; it is used of God Himself.

options.text: She is an afterthought outside God's plan.
options.note: God deliberately says it is not good for man to be alone and provides her.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the one command about the tree?

options.text: It is a real boundary of loving command that leaves room for trust and obedience.
options.note: The single prohibition amid total generosity makes love and trust possible.

options.text: It is a test of whether the man will obey God.
options.note: True, but framed only as a test it can miss God's surrounding generosity.

options.text: It shows God did not want humans to have any knowledge.
options.note: God gives freely from every other tree; the issue is trust, not knowledge itself.

options.text: It was a trap set so the man would fail.
options.note: God warns truthfully and provides abundantly; it is not a trap.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament read Adam and the garden?

options.text: Adam is a type of Christ, and marriage pictures Christ and the church.
options.note: Romans 5 and Ephesians 5 read Adam and marriage as pointing to Christ.

options.text: As history that Jesus refers to.
options.note: True, Matthew 19, but it stops short of the typology to Christ.

options.text: As a nice story with no link to Jesus.
options.note: The New Testament explicitly ties it to Christ.

options.text: As unrelated to the gospel.
options.note: Paul makes it central to the gospel of the last Adam.

quiz.q: What is the main point of Genesis 2:4 to 25?

options.text: The personal covenant God forms man for communion with Him, for work, and for marriage within His good boundary.
options.note: This is the heart of the passage.

options.text: God made a garden and the first couple.
options.note: True, but it describes the events rather than the point about God and communion.

options.text: The passage is mainly about the geography of four rivers.
options.note: The rivers are detail, not the main point.

options.text: Humans are self-made and autonomous.
options.note: The passage shows man formed, placed, and provided for by God.

questions: You were formed from dust and given life by God's breath. How does remembering that you are both earthy and God-breathed change how you see your worth today?
God gave the man real work in the garden before the fall. How can you receive your daily work this week as a gift and a calling rather than only a burden?
God set one loving boundary in a garden of freedom. Where is God's 'you may not' actually an expression of His care, and are you trusting it?
God said it is not good for the man to be alone. Who has God given you for companionship, and how might you be a strong helper to someone this week?
Marriage here points to Christ and the church. Whether married or single, how does belonging to Christ as His beloved shape your identity?

translationNotes.note: LORD in small capitals renders the covenant name YHWH, distinct from the general word God, Elohim, used in chapter one. The pairing the LORD God binds the two together.
Suitable helper renders ezer kenegdo, a helper corresponding to him. Because ezer is used of God as Israel's helper, it carries no sense of inferiority.
